Frequently Asked Questions

Answers to common questions about mediation and the Meridian process. If your question is not addressed here, the initial consultation is the best place to clarify details specific to your situation.

What is mediation?

Mediation is a voluntary, structured process in which a neutral third party — the mediator — facilitates dialogue between disputing parties. The mediator does not decide the outcome. Their role is to help parties communicate clearly, understand each other's positions, and work toward a resolution they can both accept. Any agreement reached is documented in writing and signed by the parties.

Is mediation confidential?

Yes. All sessions are conducted on a confidential basis. Nothing disclosed during mediation may be used in subsequent legal proceedings without the written agreement of all parties. There are limited legal exceptions — including situations involving imminent risk of harm — which Meridian will explain clearly at the outset of the process.

Is a mediator the same as a lawyer?

No. A mediator is a neutral facilitator, not a legal representative. Meridian does not act as counsel for any party and does not provide legal advice. You are encouraged to retain independent legal counsel to advise you throughout the process and to review any agreement before you sign it.

Do both parties need to agree to participate?

Yes. Mediation is a voluntary process. All parties must consent to participate before sessions can proceed. Meridian conducts an initial suitability assessment to determine whether the matter is appropriate for mediation and whether both parties are prepared to engage in good faith.

What happens during a mediation session?

Sessions are structured but conversational. The mediator will open with a process overview and ground rules, then provide each party with uninterrupted time to describe their situation. From there, the mediator guides discussion toward the underlying interests and concerns of each party — not just stated positions — and facilitates negotiation toward resolution. Sessions may be conducted jointly, separately (caucus format), or in a combination of both depending on the dispute.

How long does mediation take?

It depends on the complexity of the dispute. A single session typically runs two to four hours. Most matters are resolved within one to three sessions. Meridian will give you a realistic assessment of scope during the initial consultation.

What if we do not reach an agreement?

Mediation is non-binding unless a written agreement is reached and signed by all parties. If no resolution is achieved, all parties retain their full legal rights and are free to pursue other channels — including arbitration or litigation. Nothing discussed in mediation can be used against you in those proceedings.

How are fees determined?

Fees depend on the nature and complexity of the dispute, the number of sessions required, and the preparation involved. Meridian does not publish fixed rates because disputes vary significantly in scope. Fees are discussed transparently and agreed upon during the free initial consultation, before any commitment is made.

Does Meridian serve clients outside of Saskatchewan?

Yes. Meridian is based in Saskatchewan and provides mediation services virtually across Canada. In-person sessions may be available in Saskatchewan depending on the matter.

Is mediation appropriate for every dispute?

Not always. Mediation works best when all parties are willing to engage in good faith and when there is no significant power imbalance that cannot be managed within the process. During the initial consultation, Meridian will assess whether your situation is suitable for mediation and be straightforward with you if it is not.

Still have questions?

The initial consultation is free. You can ask anything, including whether mediation is appropriate for your situation.

Mediation services across Canada. Based in Saskatchewan. Not legal counsel. Not therapy.
